description:
Your company’s artifact-manager (running as an unprivileged service in  
  /home/user/artifact-manager/) will start listening on TCP port 8081 for
  HTTP/S requests.  
  Access rules are very strict:

    • Only the corporate networks 192.168.0.0/16 and 10.0.0.0/8 may reach
      port 8081.  
    • SSH (port 22) and the loopback interface must always stay open.  
    • Everything else that arrives on the INPUT chain must be dropped.  
    • The OUTPUT chain must be fully open.  
    • No FORWARD traffic is allowed.

  Instead of touching the live firewall (which would require root), you must
  prepare a persistent set of IPv4 *iptables-restore* rules and an executable
  helper script that can apply them later when a privileged operator reviews
  your work.

  Perform the following high-level steps from an interactive shell (≈ 5–10
  individual commands in total):

    1. Create a dedicated directory that will hold all firewall artefacts.
    2. Inside that directory, build a text file containing the exact
       *iptables-restore* rules shown below (including blank lines,
       asterisks, colons, dashes, upper-case words, and the trailing
       “COMMIT” line).  DO NOT add or remove anything.

         *filter
         :INPUT DROP [0:0]
         :FORWARD DROP [0:0]
         :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
         -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
         -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
         -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
         -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8081 -s 192.168.0.0/16 -j ACCEPT
         -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8081 -s 10.0.0.0/8 -j ACCEPT
         COMMIT

       File path: /home/user/firewall_configs/repo_fw_rules.v4  
       Permissions: 600

    3. Write an executable helper script that simply calls

          iptables-restore < /home/user/firewall_configs/repo_fw_rules.v4

       Script path: /home/user/firewall_configs/apply_repo_fw.sh  
       Permissions: 755

    4. Produce an operation log so the security team can see exactly which
       shell commands you issued while preparing the artefacts.

       • Create /home/user/firewall_change.log  
       • The file must contain exactly five lines.  
       • Each line must follow this precise template (no extra spaces):

           CMD1: <first command you typed>
           CMD2: <second command you typed>
           CMD3: <third command you typed>
           CMD4: <fourth command you typed>
           CMD5: <fifth command you typed>

       The automated checker will only verify the presence of the five
       labelled lines; it will NOT validate the actual commands inside them,
       so you are free to decide which five commands best complete the job.

  Summary of artefacts whose presence/content will be tested:

    1. Directory              : /home/user/firewall_configs            (must exist)
    2. Rules file             : /home/user/firewall_configs/repo_fw_rules.v4
    3. Helper script          : /home/user/firewall_configs/apply_repo_fw.sh
    4. Operations log         : /home/user/firewall_change.log

  Double-check file paths, exact rule text, permissions, and the
  five-line log format before you finish.
truth:
Expected filesystem state after successful completion:

  1. /home/user/firewall_configs      → directory, mode 755

  2. /home/user/firewall_configs/repo_fw_rules.v4  
     • mode 600  
     • contents EXACTLY (ten lines, including the blank line after *filter*):

       *filter
       :INPUT DROP [0:0]
       :FORWARD DROP [0:0]
       :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
       -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
       -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8081 -s 192.168.0.0/16 -j ACCEPT
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8081 -s 10.0.0.0/8 -j ACCEPT
       COMMIT

  3. /home/user/firewall_configs/apply_repo_fw.sh  
     • mode 755  
     • first line: #!/bin/bash  
     • second line: iptables-restore < /home/user/firewall_configs/repo_fw_rules.v4  
       (absolute path, single space before “<”)

  4. /home/user/firewall_change.log  
     • exactly five lines  
     • each line must match regex: ^CMD[1-5]:\ .+$
